narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as an uphill battle—specifically, an 81% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest thread pulling everything toward that tough outcome was your Year 3 decile of 2, placing you near the bottom of your class and signaling to the model a real struggle with the rigors of advanced legal coursework. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 25, which stood out as another major flag, whispering that the raw analytical skills needed for bar success might not quite match what's typically required. Your Year 1 decile of 2 echoed this early pattern, suggesting these challenges weren't a one-off but a consistent hurdle in keeping pace academically. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation for many, tipped toward risk in this model's view, perhaps reflecting patterns where that bracket doesn't always translate to the extra edges needed here. Your Asian background added another subtle pull in the same direction, based on historical trends the model has learned. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 continued the theme of steady but not standout performance, while committing to full-time studies highlighted the intensity ahead without much buffer. The one bright note was your gender, which nudged things slightly toward success in the model's eyes. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone resilient and determined—like you, pursuing this full-time—but facing a steep climb against odds stacked by grades and test scores that haven't yet broken through the top tiers. It's not a judgment on your potential or heart; it's just the story these numbers told. If you boost those deciles or LSAT in retakes, your narrative could shift dramatically—keep fighting, because stories like yours often have the best twists.
